name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
مَّا يَفْتَحِ ٱللَّهُ لِلنَّاسِ مِن رَّحْمَةٍ فَلَا مُمْسِكَ لَهَا ۖ وَمَا يُمْسِكْ فَلَا مُرْسِلَ لَهُۥ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (٢)
```

```english
Whatever mercy Allah unfolds for the people, no one can withhold it; and whatever He withholds no one can release it except Him, and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise. (2)
```

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ ٱذْكُرُوا۟ نِعْمَتَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ ۚ هَلْ مِنْ خَـٰلِقٍ غَيْرُ ٱللَّهِ يَرْزُقُكُم مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ فَأَنَّىٰ تُؤْفَكُونَ (٣)
```

```english
O mankind! Remember Allah’s blessing upon you! Is there any creator other than Allah who provides for you from the sky and the earth? There is no god except Him. So where do you stray? (3)
```

```arabic
وَإِن يُكَذِّبُوكَ فَقَدْ كُذِّبَتْ رُسُلٌ مِّن قَبْلِكَ ۚ وَإِلَى ٱللَّهِ تُرْجَعُ ٱلْأُمُورُ (٤)
```

```english
If they impugn you, certainly [other] apostles were impugned before you, and all matters are returned to Allah. (4)
```

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ إِنَّ وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقٌّ ۖ فَلَا تَغُرَّنَّكُمُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ وَلَا يَغُرَّنَّكُم بِٱللَّهِ ٱلْغَرُورُ (٥)
```

```english
O mankind! Allah’s promise is indeed true. So do not let the life of the world deceive you, nor let the Deceiver deceive you concerning Allah. (5)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنَ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ فَٱتَّخِذُوهُ عَدُوًّا ۚ إِنَّمَا يَدْعُوا۟ حِزْبَهُۥ لِيَكُونُوا۟ مِنْ أَصْحَـٰبِ ٱلسَّعِيرِ (٦)
```

```english
Satan is indeed your enemy, so treat him as an enemy. He only invites his confederates so that they may be among the inmates of the Blaze. (6)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ شَدِيدٌ ۖ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ لَهُم مَّغْفِرَةٌ وَأَجْرٌ كَبِيرٌ (٧)
```

```english
There is a severe punishment for the faithless; but for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, there will be forgiveness and a great reward. (7)
```

```arabic
أَفَمَن زُيِّنَ لَهُۥ سُوٓءُ عَمَلِهِۦ فَرَءَاهُ حَسَنًا ۖ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُضِلُّ مَن يَشَآءُ وَيَهْدِى مَن يَشَآءُ ۖ فَلَا تَذْهَبْ نَفْسُكَ عَلَيْهِمْ حَسَرَٰتٍ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلِيمٌۢ بِمَا يَصْنَعُونَ (٨)
```

```english
Is someone the evil of whose conduct is presented as decorous to him, so he regards it as good.... Indeed Allah leads astray whomever He wishes, and guides whomever He wishes. So do not fret yourself to death regretting for them. Indeed Allah knows best what they do. (8)
```

## (Commentary)

After the author alluded to the angels, who are intermediaries in the arrival of blessings to the creation, he now points toward the blessings themselves in a universal manner. He mentions that all blessings are from Allah, the Glorified, and none other; for He is the Provider (الرازق) who has no partner in this regard. He then uses the attribute of providing (الرازقية) as an argument for His Lordship (الربوبية), and subsequently for the Resurrection (المعاد), asserting that His promise of the rising, the punishment of the disbelievers, and the forgiveness of the righteous believers is the truth. Within these verses is also a consolation for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

His saying, the Exalted: **مَا يَفْتَحِ اللَّهُ لِلنَّاسِ مِنْ رَحْمَةٍ فَلَا مُمْسِكَ لَهَا وَمَا يُمْسِكْ فَلَا مُرْسِلَ لَهُ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ** — "Whatever mercy Allah unfolds for the people, no one can withhold it; and whatever He withholds, no one can release it after Him" (35:2). The meaning is that whatever blessing Allah grants to people—which is sustenance (الرزق)—there is none to prevent it.

And whatever He prevents, there is none to grant it. The apparent requirement of the context would have been to say: "Whatever Allah sends (يرسل) to the people...", just as He used the term "release/sending" (الإرسال) in the second clause. However, He shifted from "sending" to "unfolding/opening" (الفتح) because it occurs repeatedly in His speech that His mercy has treasuries (خزائن), such as His saying: **أَمْ عِنْدَهُمْ خَزَائِنُ رَحْمَةِ رَبِّكَ الْعَزِيزِ الْوَهَّابِ** — "Do they possess the treasuries of the mercy of your Lord, the All-mighty, the All-munificent?" (38:9), and His saying: **قُلْ لَوْ أَنْتُمْ تَمْلِكُونَ خَزَائِنَ رَحْمَةِ رَبِّي إِذًا لَأَمْسَكْتُمْ خَشْيَةَ الْإِنْفَاقِ** — "Say, ‘If you possessed the treasuries of my Lord’s mercy, you would surely withhold them for fear of spending’" (17:100). The expression of "opening" (الفتح) is more appropriate than "sending" when referring to treasuries. Thus, there is an indication that the mercy granted to people is stored in treasuries that encompass them; their attaining it depends on nothing but its being opened, without any additional burden.

Sustenance (الرزق), which is the blessing (النعمة), has been expressed as mercy (الرحمة) to demonstrate that His bestowing of these blessings arises from pure mercy, without any expectation of a benefit returning to Him or a perfection by which He might complete Himself.

And His saying: **وَمَا يُمْسِكْ فَلَا مُرْسِلَ لَهُ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ** — "and whatever He withholds, no one can release it after Him." That is, whatever He prevents of mercy, there is no releaser for it besides Him. In the expression "after Him" (من بعده), there is an indication that He, the Exalted, is the First in withholding just as He is the First in giving.

And His saying: **وَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ** — "and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise." This is an affirmation of the ruling mentioned in the noble verse through these two noble Names. Because He, the Exalted, is the All-mighty (العزيز), He is never overcome when He gives; thus, no withholder can withhold from Him. And when He withholds, no giver can give. Furthermore, He, the Exalted, is the All-wise (الحكيم); when He gives, He gives out of wisdom (حكمة) and purpose (مصلحة), and when He withholds, He withholds out of wisdom and purpose. In short, there is no giver but Allah and no withholder but He, and His withholding and giving are based on wisdom.

His saying, the Exalted: **يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ اذْكُرُوا نِعْمَتَ اللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ هَلْ مِنْ خَالِقٍ غَيْرُ اللَّهِ يَرْزُقُكُمْ مِنَ السَّمَاءِ وَالْأَرْضِ** — "O mankind! Remember Allah’s blessing upon you! Is there any creator other than Allah who provides for you from the sky and the earth?" (35:3). Since the previous verse established that giving and withholding belong to Allah, the Glorified, and no one shares that with Him, He uses that in this verse as an argument for His uniqueness in Lordship (الربوبية).

The formulation of the argument is that a deity is only a worshipped deity because of his Lordship, which is the faculty of managing the affairs (تدبير أمر) of people and others. The one who possesses the management of affairs through these blessings—in which people and others fluctuate and by which they are sustained—is Allah, the Glorified, to the exclusion of the other deities they have taken. This is because He, the Glorified, is the one who created them, while the others did not; and creation (الخلق) is inseparable from management (التدبير) and never departs from it. Thus, He, the Glorified, is your God; there is no god but He, because He is your Lord who manages your affairs through these blessings in which you fluctuate. He is only a managing Lord through these blessings because He is their Creator and the Creator of the system (النظام) that governs them.

By this, it becomes clear that the "people" (الناس) addressed are the idolaters and others who have taken a partner with Allah.

And His saying: **اذْكُرُوا نِعْمَتَ اللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ** — "Remember Allah’s blessing upon you." What is meant by remembrance (الذكر) here is the opposite of forgetfulness (النسيان), not merely verbal remembrance.

And His saying: **هَلْ مِنْ خَالِقٍ غَيْرُ اللَّهِ يَرْزُقُكُمْ مِنَ السَّمَاءِ وَالْأَرْضِ** — "Is there any creator other than Allah who provides for you from the sky and the earth?" Sustenance (الرزق) is that which aids in the preservation of existence. Its source is the sky, by means of rays, rain, and other things; and the earth, by means of plants, animals, and other things.

By this, it also becomes clear that there is a subtle conciseness in the verse. Mercy (الرحمة) in the previous verse was replaced by blessing (النعمة) in this verse first, and then blessing was replaced by sustenance (الرزق) second. The requirement of the context of the two verses would have been to say: "Is there any provider (رازق)..." or "Is there any benefactor (منعم)..." or "Is there any merciful one (راحم)..." but instead, it was replaced by His saying: **هَلْ مِنْ خَالِقٍ** — "Is there any creator...". This serves as an allusion to a second proof that cuts off all dispute. For they [the polytheists] believed that the management of the world belonged to their deities by Allah's permission. Had it been said: "Is there any provider or benefactor other than Allah?", the dispute would not have been settled, as they could have said: "Yes, our deities, through the delegation of management from Allah to them." However, when it was said: "Is there any creator...", the description pointed out that the Provider and Manager is the Creator of the sustenance and none other. Thus, the dispute was severed, and they could only respond by denying any creator other than Allah who provides for them from the sky and the earth.

And His saying: **لَا إِلَهَ إِلَّا هُوَ** — "There is no god but He." This is a parenthetical statement of monotheism (التوحيد) that serves to glorify Him, similar to His saying: **وَقَالُوا اتَّخَذَ اللَّهُ وَلَدًا سُبْحَانَهُ** — "And they say, ‘Allah has taken a son.’ Immaculate is He!" (2:116).

That is, there is no deity truly worthy of worship except Him, because the one entitled to worship is the one who bestows blessings upon you and provides for you, and that is none but Allah.

And His saying: **فَأَنَّى تُؤْفَكُونَ** — "So where are you being led astray?" This is a reproach branching from the preceding proof. That is: if the matter is as such and you acknowledge it, then until when will you be turned away from the truth toward falsehood, and from monotheism toward polytheism (الإشراك)?

Regarding the grammatical analysis of the verse, namely His saying: **هَلْ مِنْ خَالِقٍ غَيْرُ اللَّهِ** — "Is there any creator other than Allah...", there have been long disputes among the grammarians. What suits the aforementioned establishment of the proof is that "min" (مِنْ) is extra (زائدة) for the sake of universality (التعميم). His saying: **غَيْرُ اللَّهِ** — "other than Allah" is an adjective for "creator" (خالق) following its grammatical place (المحل). Likewise, His saying: **يَرْزُقُكُمْ** — "who provides for you...". And "from a creator" (مِنْ خَالِقٍ) is a subject (مبتدأ) whose predicate (الخبر) is omitted, being "exists" (موجود). His saying: **لَا إِلَهَ إِلَّا هُوَ** — "There is no god but He" is a parenthesis, and His saying: **فَأَنَّى تُؤْفَكُونَ** — "So where are you being led astray?" is a derivation from what preceded it.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِنْ يُكَذِّبُوكَ فَقَدْ كُذِّبَتْ رُسُلٌ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ وَإِلَى اللَّهِ تُرْجَعُ الْأُمُورُ** — "If they impugn you, then messengers were already impugned before you, and to Allah all matters are returned" (35:4). This is a consolation for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). That is: if they deny you after hearing these radiant proofs, do not grieve, for this is not unprecedented; messengers before you were denied by their nations and peoples. To Allah all matters are returned, and He will recompense them as they deserve for their denial of the truth after its manifestation; they cannot frustrate Him by their denial.

From here it becomes clear that His saying: **فَقَدْ كُذِّبَتْ رُسُلٌ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ** — "then messengers were already impugned before you" is an instance of placing the cause in the position of the effect, and that His saying: **وَإِلَى اللَّهِ تُرْجَعُ الْأُمُورُ** — "and to Allah all matters are returned" is coordinated with His saying: "then messengers were already impugned...".

His saying, the Exalted: **يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنَّ وَعْدَ اللَّهِ حَقٌّ فَلَا تَغُرَّنَّكُمُ الْحَيَاةُ الدُّنْيَا وَلَا يَغُرَّنَّكُمْ بِاللَّهِ الْغَرُورُ** — "O mankind! Allah’s promise is indeed true. So do not let the life of the world deceive you, nor let the Deceiver deceive you concerning Allah" (35:5). This is a general address to mankind reminding them of the Resurrection (المعاد), just as the previous general address reminded them of His uniqueness in Lordship and Divinity (الألوهية).

So His saying: **إِنَّ وَعْدَ اللَّهِ حَقٌّ** — "Allah’s promise is indeed true." That is, His promise that He will resurrect you and recompense every worker for his deed, whether good or evil, is "true" (حق), meaning established and certain to occur. He has explicitly stated this promise in His upcoming saying: **الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ شَدِيدٌ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ لَهُمْ مَغْفِرَةٌ وَأَجْرٌ كَبِيرٌ** — "For the faithless there is a severe punishment, and for those who have faith and do righteous deeds there is forgiveness and a great reward" (35:7).

And His saying: **فَلَا تَغُرَّنَّكُمُ الْحَيَاةُ الدُّنْيَا** — "So do not let the life of the world deceive you." Although the prohibition is directed toward the worldly life in form, it is in reality directed toward them. The meaning is: if Allah's promise is true, then do not be deceived by the worldly life through preoccupation with its adornment and being diverted by its pleasures and amusements that make you forget the Day of Reckoning, nor by being engrossed in seeking it and turning away from the truth.

And His saying: **وَلَا يَغُرَّنَّكُمْ بِاللَّهِ الْغَرُورُ** — "nor let the Deceiver deceive you concerning Allah." The word "Deceiver" (الغرور) with a *fatha* on the *ghayn* is an intensive form (صيغة مبالغة) of deception (الغرور) with a *damma*, referring to one who is excessive in deceiving and whose habit it is. It is apparent—as has been said—that what is meant by it is Satan (الشيطان). This is supported by the justification given in the following verse: **إِنَّ الشَّيْطَانَ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ** — "Indeed Satan is your enemy..." (35:6).

The meaning of his deceiving them "concerning Allah" is his directing their attention sometimes to the manifestations of His forbearance (حلم) and pardon (عفو), and at other times to the manifestations of His trial (ابتلاء), gradual leading to ruin (استدراج), and scheme (كيد). Thus, they see that preoccupation with the world, forgetting the Hereafter, and turning away from the truth and reality does not result in immediate punishment or entail accountability. They see that the children of the world, the more they persist in their pursuit, plunge into their heedlessness, and immerse themselves in sins, the more their living becomes pleasant, their life comfortable, and their status and honor among people increase. At that point, Satan casts into their hearts that there is no nobility except in advancing in the worldly life, and no news of what lies beyond it; and that the promise and threat contained in the true call, and the resurrection, reckoning, Paradise, and Fire reported by prophecy, are nothing but myths.

Thus, what is meant by Satan's deceiving man "concerning Allah" is man's being deceived by the way Allah treats man despite his heedlessness and injustice.

It has also been said that "the Deceiver" (الغرور) refers to the world itself which deceives man, and that His saying: **وَلَا يَغُرَّنَّكُمْ بِاللَّهِ الْغَرُورُ** is an emphasis of His saying: **فَلَا تَغُرَّنَّكُمُ الْحَيَاةُ الدُّنْيَا** by repeating its meaning.

His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ الشَّيْطَانَ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ فَاتَّخِذُوهُ عَدُوًّا** — "Indeed Satan is your enemy; so treat him as an enemy" (35:6). This is a justification for the preceding prohibition in His saying: **وَلَا يَغُرَّنَّكُمْ بِاللَّهِ الْغَرُورُ**. What is meant by Satan's enmity is that he has no concern except to seduce man and deprive him of the happiness of life and a good end. What is meant by "treating Satan as an enemy" is to avoid following his call to falsehood and not to obey him in what he suggests through his whisperings (وساوس) and allurements (تسويلات). For this reason, He justified his enmity by saying: **إِنَّمَا يَدْعُو حِزْبَهُ** — "He only invites his confederates...".

So His saying: **إِنَّمَا يَدْعُو حِزْبَهُ لِيَكُونُوا مِنْ أَصْحابِ السَّعِيرِ** — "He only invites his confederates that they may be among the inmates of the Blaze" is in the position of justifying what preceded it. A "confederacy" (الحزب) is a group of people united by a single purpose. The "lam" in "that they may be" (ليكونوا) is for causality (التعليل); thus, their being among the inmates of the Blaze is the final cause (علة غائية) of his invitation. The "Blaze" (السعير) is the kindled fire, and it is one of the names of Hell (جهنم) in the Quran.

His saying, the Exalted: **الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ شَدِيدٌ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ لَهُمْ مَغْفِرَةٌ وَأَجْرٌ كَبِيرٌ** — "For the faithless there is a severe punishment, and for those who have faith and do righteous deeds there is forgiveness and a great reward" (35:7). This is the true promise mentioned by Allah, the Glorified. The indefiniteness (تنكير) of "punishment" (عذاب) is to indicate its magnitude (التفخيم), and also that they have different levels (دركات) and ranks of punishment according to their disbelief and wickedness; thus, ambiguity is more appropriate. A similar two-fold reasoning applies to His saying: "forgiveness and a reward" (مغفرة وأجر).

His saying, the Exalted: **أَفَمَنْ زُيِّنَ لَهُ سُوءُ عَمَلِهِ فَرَآهُ حَسَنًا فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ يُضِلُّ مَنْ يَشَاءُ وَيَهْدِي مَنْ يَشَاءُ** — "Is he the evil of whose deeds is presented as decorous to him, so he regards it as good [like one who is guided]? Indeed Allah leads astray whomever He wishes and guides whomever He wishes" (35:8). This is an affirmation and explanation of the division contained in the previous verse—namely, the division of people into a disbeliever who has a severe punishment and a believer who does righteous deeds and has forgiveness and a great reward. The meaning is that the two are not equal, nor is the outcome of their affairs equal.

So His saying: **أَفَمَنْ زُيِّنَ لَهُ سُوءُ عَمَلِهِ فَرَآهُ حَسَنًا** — "Is he the evil of whose deeds is presented as decorous to him, so he regards it as good..." is a subject (مبتدأ) whose predicate (خبر) is omitted, i.e., "...like one who is not so?". The "fa" (ف) is to branch the sentence from the meaning of the previous verse, and the interrogative is for denial (الإنكار). What is meant by "he the evil of whose deeds is presented as decorous to him, so he regards it as good" is the disbeliever. He points out by this that his understanding is inverted and his intellect is overcome, seeing his deed as other than what it truly is. The meaning is that he whose evil deed is beautified for him so that he sees it as good is not equal to the one who is not like that, but rather sees the evil as evil.

And His saying: **فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ يُضِلُّ مَنْ يَشَاءُ وَيَهْدِي مَنْ يَشَاءُ** — "Indeed Allah leads astray whomever He wishes and guides whomever He wishes" is a justification for the preceding denial in His saying: "Is he the evil of whose deeds...". That is: the disbeliever who is such, and the believer who is the opposite, are not equal because Allah leads one of them astray by His will—and that is the disbeliever who sees the evil as good—and He guides the other by His will—and that is the believer who does righteous deeds and sees the evil as evil.

This leading astray (الإضلال) is a leading astray by way of recompense (المجازاة) and is not an initial leading astray (إضلال ابتدائي); thus, there is no harm in attributing it to Allah, the Glorified.

In short, the difference between the disbeliever and the believer in their outcomes, according to the divine promise of punishment and mercy, is due to their difference in divine leading astray and guidance; and their difference in leading astray and guidance is due to their difference in seeing the evil as good or otherwise.

And His saying: **فَلَا تَذْهَبْ نَفْسُكَ عَلَيْهِمْ حَسَرَاتٍ** — "so do not let yourself be consumed by regrets for them." Regrets (الحسرات) is the plural of regret (حسرة), which is grief over what has passed and remorse for it. It is in the accusative case because it is an object of purpose (مفعول لأجله). What is meant by the "going of the soul" (ذهاب النفس) over them is its perishing because of the regrets arising from their lack of faith.

The sentence is branched from the preceding distinction. That is: if the two groups are different in terms of leading astray and guidance from Allah's side, then do not let your soul perish in regrets over them because they denied you and disbelieved in you. For Allah is the one who leads them astray as a recompense for their disbelief and their seeing the evil as good, and He is All-knowing of what they do; thus, the matter is not confused for Him, and He does not do with them except what is right, nor does He recompense them except with justice.

From here it becomes clear that His saying: **إِنَّ اللَّهَ عَلِيمٌ بِمَا يَصْنَعُونَ** — "Indeed Allah knows best what they do" is in the position of justifying His saying: **فَلَا تَذْهَبْ نَفْسُكَ عَلَيْهِمْ حَسَرَاتٍ**. It is not appropriate for the Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to let his soul perish in regrets over them when they have gone astray and the word of punishment has become due against them, for Allah is the one who leads them astray because of their conduct, and He is All-knowing of what they do.
